problem_summary: 2021 ICM Problem D (Music Influence Networks) — NOTE: the corpus label "Problem D / operations_research" is a misclassification; this is the music/network problem. Team 2124668 ("Sonic & Iconic") was tasked with quantifying artist influence over ~90 years using two datasets: an artist-to-artist influence file (5854 artists, directed influencer→follower edges) and a per-artist sonic-features file (13 Spotify-style audio variables). The team had to (1) rank influential artists, (2) build a music-similarity metric and test whether sound predicts genre, (3) identify musical "revolutions" and the "revolutionaries" who caused them, and (4) tie those revolutions back to real music history. They concluded Frank Ocean was the most influential 21st-century artist and that sound is a weak predictor of established genre, motivating their own sound-derived "Sonic Genres."
models_used: Directed unweighted influence network N (node=artist, edge influencer->follower; 5854 artists, one giant component + two 2-node components, mostly acyclic — only ~4% of Pop/Rock in cycles)

Katz centrality (Eq.1) chosen over eigenvector centrality (fails on near-acyclic directed graphs) and PageRank (down-weights high in-degree = artists with many influences); decay alpha bounded by 1/lambda_max(A), with alpha_max = 1/lambda_Tot as the scaling unit

Time-scaled Katz centrality (Eq.2): K_tilde = 1/(2020-decade) * exp(-K), assuming indirect influence accumulates exponentially in time; two operating regimes alpha_1=0.2*alpha_max (Direct) and alpha_2=1.0*alpha_max (Indirect)
Genre influence entropy S(v) (Eq.3) — adapted from Bryan & Wang's 'genre entropy', a Shannon-entropy 'spread of influence' across genres

4-component Influence Vector InfVec(v) (Eq.4): [direct KC, indirect KC, in-genre direct KC, genre entropy], equally normalized, ranked by Euclidean norm; later augmented to 5 components (Eq.10) adding direct KC within Sonic Genre
Sonic feature engineering: dropped 'explicit' (value judgment) and 'key' (non-linear/non-metric), kept 'mode'; MinMax-normalized 11 variables into an 11-dim vector space

Three candidate distance/similarity metrics: Euclidean (with similarity mu=(1+d)^-1, Eq.5), Manhattan (Eq.6), Cosine; Euclidean chosen and defended against curse-of-dimensionality via Hinneburg et al.

Feed-forward Keras Sequential neural net (11->8->4 dense), categorical cross-entropy + Adam, class weights for imbalance — used as a CLASSIFIER and as a CLUSTER VALIDATOR (70/30 split, best of 10 trials)
K-means clustering (scikit-learn, SSE objective Eq.7) on 11 sonic dims to define 4 'Sonic Genres' (named 'Here Comes the Sun', 'Thunderstruck', 'I Will Survive', 'Swan Lake')
Adjusted Rand Index (Eq.8-9) to compare Sonic-Genre vs Established-Genre partitions (ARI=0.161); swept k=3..20 to justify k=4 maximizes ARI
Directed modularity Q_P (Eq.11) to test partition quality on the influence graph: Q_G=0.38 (established), Q_Gamma=0.17 (sonic), Q_G'=0.31 (top-4 established)

Popularity-weighted genre/mainstream centroids over time; revolutions = large Euclidean shift of centroid across a 10-yr window (mainstream, established-genre, sonic-genre) plus 'mainstreaming' revolutions = sudden drop in centroid-to-mainstream distance
Sonic 'contagiousness' metric (Eq.12): edges / sum|x_influencer - x_follower| per variable — found speechiness, duration, tempo, loudness most contagious
Revolutionary scoring: normalized direct Katz centrality * similarity(artist sonic point, post-revolution centroid endpoint), per revolution type
key_techniques: Method selection by elimination with stated failure modes: explicitly rejected out-degree, eigenvector centrality, and PageRank with the specific reason each fails on this graph, then justified Katz — this reads like a literature-grounded engineering decision, not a default

Tying a modeling parameter to a physical assumption: the exp/time-scaling in Eq.2 is derived from the explicit assumption that indirect influence accumulates exponentially, and the two alpha regimes are read off the data (Fig.3) rather than hand-set

Turning a model FAILURE into a contribution: the NN's inability to predict established genre from sound (0% then 63% only on top-4 genres) is reframed as the central insight that 'sound != genre,' which motivates the original Sonic-Genres construct — the paper's signature move
Reusing one neural net for two jobs: first as a genre classifier, then as an unsupervised-cluster VALIDATOR (52% vs 25% chance baseline proves the K-means clusters are non-arbitrary)
Composite vector + Euclidean-norm aggregation (InfVec) to fuse four incommensurable influence notions into a single defensible ranking, with equal weighting justified as 'avoiding subjectivity'
Hyperparameter justification by triangulation: k=4 for K-means is independently supported by (a) top-4 genres = 80% of data, (b) NN accuracy, and (c) ARI maximization across k=3..20
Subnetwork scoping to fix a sensitivity problem: restricting to top-5 genres and post-2000 artists to stop fringe genres (e.g., 4-artist Children's Music) from dominating in-genre Katz scores

Centroid-trajectory analysis: representing an entire genre/era as a popularity-weighted point in sonic space and detecting 'revolutions' as large displacements, with explicit thresholds (3.0, 4.0, 0.2) tuned to surface a few clean peaks

Closing the loop with ground truth: every detected revolution is cross-checked against documented music history (golden age of radio 1930s, British Invasion 1960s, internet/electronic 2000s), and unmatched revolutions are argued to be the model's novel discoveries
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on three axes. First, METHODOLOGICAL RIGOR with a narrative spine: nearly every choice (Katz over PageRank, Euclidean over weighted metrics, k=4, the post-2000 subnetwork) is motivated by a stated shortcoming of the alternative and cited to literature (Bryan & Wang, Hinneburg, Serra), so the model never feels arbitrary. Second, ORIGINALITY that emerges organically: the 'Sonic Genres' idea is not bolted on — it is the consequence of an honest negative result (the NN can't predict genre from sound), and the team then validates the new construct quantitatively (NN at 52% vs 25% chance, ARI maximized at k=4, modularity Q=0.17). Judges reward a model that surprises and then proves the surprise is real. Third, SYNTHESIS AND CLOSURE: the revolution/revolutionary analysis reuses every earlier metric (Katz, entropy, centroids, similarity), then validates against real history and even claims a genuine DISCOVERY (the 1960s 'I Will Survive' sonic revolution driven by R&B artists like Barry White, invisible to genre-centric historiography). It answers every sub-question, includes proper sensitivity and error analysis that names its own weaknesses, and the writing is confident and memorable. That combination — defensible math, an original validated idea, full-loop synthesis, and self-aware honesty — is exactly the O-award profile.
section_highlights: Memorable framing and structure: a punchy title ("SONIC & ICONIC"), section epigraphs from Kanye, The Beatles, and Edgar Cayce, and a genuinely witty "Letter to ICM" (addressed to the 'Integrative Collective Music Society') that translates every technical result into fan-facing language ("quantitative evidence supporting their complaints that their favorite genre has become too mainstream"). A clean Table of Notation (Table 1) maps every symbol to its defining equation. Equations are numbered and referenced consistently (Eq.1-12). The Strengths/Weaknesses split (8.1/8.2) is unusually candid — it admits the revolution thresholds were tuned to yield clean peaks and that the history-matching in 5.7 has confirmation bias. The Sonic-Genre centroid table (Table 4) uses red/green shading to make each genre's signature instantly readable. The summary page front-loads the four headline findings (Frank Ocean; sound!=genre; four revolution types; revolutions align with political/social/tech change) so a judge knows the payload in 30 seconds.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Threshold cherry-picking: revolution thresholds (3.0, 4.0, 0.2) were explicitly set to 'yield a few clear peaks' — the team admits this is subjective and untested, so the revolutions are partly artifacts of threshold choice

Confirmation bias in validation: 5.7 starts from a detected revolution and searches for a matching historical event rather than predicting events blind; they admit the honest test (what fraction of historically known revolutions does the model recover?) was not done

Contagiousness confound: speechiness/duration are flagged as most 'contagious,' but the team notes these are likely constant within a genre, so the metric may be re-measuring genre rather than influence transmission

Weak/unvalidated ML: best-of-10-trials accuracy reporting inflates results; the genre classifier only works after collapsing to 4 genres; the data text even shows a '0.0% accuracy' artifact and several OCR-garbled figures suggest fragile reproducibility
Katz cross-network comparison is ad hoc: they swap the constant '1' in Eq.1 for |N| 'mostly based on quality of results' and admit a principled normalization is missing

k=4 partly circular: K-means k=4 is justified by ARI to established genres and by the 4-genre NN result, so the 'sound-derived' clusters are implicitly anchored to the established-genre labels they claim to be agnostic of
Data coverage gaps acknowledged: Hip-Hop and Neo-Soul absent from the data despite driving the 1990s R&B revolution they analyze, undermining that case study's causal story
No ground-truth validation possible for the headline outputs (most-influential, revolutionaries) — the team candidly concedes these lists cannot be checked against any reference
Girvan-Newman 'Influence Genres' abandoned for being too computationally expensive — a richer comparison was left undone
reusable_patterns: Negative-result-as-feature pattern: when a model fails (NN can't predict genre from sound), reframe the failure as the motivating insight for an original construct, then VALIDATE the new construct quantitatively (chance baseline, ARI, modularity). High-value O-award move.

Method-selection-by-elimination: before adopting an algorithm, name 2-3 alternatives and the specific property of THIS dataset that disqualifies each (acyclicity kills eigenvector centrality; in-degree penalty kills PageRank). Cite literature for each claim.

Composite-metric vector + norm: fuse several incommensurable scores into one ranking by normalizing each to [0,1] and taking the Euclidean norm, justifying equal weights as the only non-arbitrary choice. Lets you answer 'who is most X' defensibly.

Multi-evidence hyperparameter justification: pin a hyperparameter (k for clustering) with at least three independent signals (data coverage %, supervised accuracy, partition-similarity index swept over a range) instead of one elbow plot.

Centroid-trajectory + threshold for change detection: represent a population as a weighted centroid in feature space and detect 'events' as large displacements over a fixed window; disclose the threshold explicitly and its tuning.
Reuse one classifier as a cluster validator: train a supervised model to predict your unsupervised cluster labels; beating the chance baseline is evidence the clusters are real, not arbitrary.

Close the loop against external ground truth: map every model-detected event to documented real-world events, and explicitly label the unmatched ones as candidate discoveries (while flagging the confirmation-bias risk).

Subnetwork scoping to control sensitivity: when a global metric is dominated by tiny subpopulations, restrict to a principled subset (top-k by size, recent time window) and document this in the sensitivity section.

Communication wrappers: a named/themed paper identity, section epigraphs, color-shaded result tables, and a plain-language stakeholder letter that re-states every quantitative finding in the audience's vocabulary.
Honest Strengths/Weaknesses section that names your own threshold tuning and validation bias — judges trust self-aware papers more than ones that oversell.
